summary: Patrick McKenzie announces the official 1.0.0 release of A/Bingo, his open-source Rails A/B testing framework. The release includes several community-requested features: a default dashboard, the ability to stop experiments, named conversions, Redis support, and a proper test suite. He establishes a versioning policy for the growing user base and closes with his characteristic evangelism for A/B testing as an essential business practice.
key_points: A/Bingo has grown beyond personal use to substantial community adoption, with McKenzie's own users representing less than 1% of total beneficiaries
The release prioritizes community-requested features like a default dashboard and experiment stopping, reflecting the shift from personal tool to public infrastructure
McKenzie added unit tests not because he personally values them but because the Rails community expects them, showing pragmatic responsiveness to ecosystem norms
Named conversions reduce the code needed to create most new tests from two lines to one, reflecting a DRY-focused design philosophy
The versioning policy signals maturity — acknowledging that breaking changes now affect real deployments beyond his own
money_quotes: The 5,000 or so people seeing A/B tests on my site on Valentine's Day are almost certainly less than 1% of the beneficiaries of the software now.
I want to make your use of the software as pleasant as possible to further evangelize the cause of A/B testing.
Unit tests are not exactly my cup of tea ("Argh, it works in production, what else do you want from me?!"), but Rails developers look askance at software that does not include them.
By default, it probably looks terrible. If you want to send me a patch to make it pretty, be my guest.
A/B testing is an easy, reproducible process that you can use to improve your marketing, website copy, product, user experience, etc.
It won't take you five minutes until you're getting actionable data which you can use to make money.
key_insight: Open-source developer tools succeed when they shift from solving the author's problems to systematically addressing community friction points like documentation, dashboards, and testing expectations.
